clean_output:
summary: Developer-written repository context files provided a modest performance gain over no context, while LLM-generated context usually hurt performance and both kinds raised cost.
claims:
  - claim_id: claim-001
    claim_text: On AGENTbench, developer-provided repository context files improved coding-agent performance by 4% on average relative to omitting context files.
    claim_kind: empirical_claim
    evidence_ids:
      - evidence-02
      - evidence-01
    mechanism_or_bounds: The evidence supports an average performance gain on a 138-instance benchmark drawn from 12 repositories, but it does not establish the causal mechanism behind that gain.
    contradiction_note: No direct contradiction is visible for this comparison, though the gain is bounded to the evaluated benchmark and repositories.
    confidence: medium
  - claim_id: claim-002
    claim_text: Across the evaluated settings, LLM-generated repository context files generally reduced coding-agent performance rather than improving it.
    claim_kind: empirical_claim
    evidence_ids:
      - evidence-03
      - evidence-06
      - evidence-07
    mechanism_or_bounds: The evidence shows lower average resolution rates and worse outcomes in 5 of 8 settings, but it does not identify why generated context harmed performance.
    contradiction_note: There is visible tension because one ablation found LLM-generated context improved performance when other repository documentation was removed.
    confidence: medium
  - claim_id: claim-003
    claim_text: Repository context files increased agent cost materially, with measured increases above 20% in the evaluated settings.
    claim_kind: bounded_claim
    evidence_ids:
      - evidence-04
      - evidence-09
      - evidence-11
    mechanism_or_bounds: The most supported mechanism is indirect and bounded: context files were associated with more agent steps, which likely contributed to higher cost, but the evidence does not fully decompose the cost increase.
    contradiction_note: No direct contradiction is visible on the direction of cost change; the main limit is that the size of the increase varies by context-file source and benchmark.
    confidence: high
  - claim_id: claim-004
    claim_text: The apparent usefulness of repository context depends on information conditions: developer-written files beat LLM-generated files in the main evaluations, but generated files helped when other documentation was removed.
    claim_kind: comparative_claim
    evidence_ids:
      - evidence-10
      - evidence-14
    mechanism_or_bounds: The evidence supports a conditional interpretation rather than a general mechanism: generated context may recover missing repository guidance in sparse-documentation settings, but this does not generalize to the main benchmark conditions.
    contradiction_note: This claim carries explicit tension: the main evaluations favor developer-written context across all four agents, while the documentation-removal ablation reverses that ordering.

summary: Developer-written repository context files provided a modest performance gain over no context, while LLM-generated context usually hurt performance and both kinds raised cost.
claims:
  - claim_id: claim-001
    claim_text: On AGENTbench, developer-provided repository context files improved coding-agent performance by 4% on average relative to omitting context files.
    claim_kind: empirical_claim
    evidence_ids:
      - evidence-02
      - evidence-01
    mechanism_or_bounds: The evidence supports an average performance gain on a 138-instance benchmark drawn from 12 repositories, but it does not establish the causal mechanism behind that gain.
    contradiction_note: No direct contradiction is visible for this comparison, though the gain is bounded to the evaluated benchmark and repositories.
    confidence: medium
  - claim_id: claim-002
    claim_text: Across the evaluated settings, LLM-generated repository context files generally reduced coding-agent performance rather than improving it.
    claim_kind: empirical_claim
    evidence_ids:
      - evidence-03
      - evidence-06
      - evidence-07
    mechanism_or_bounds: The evidence shows lower average resolution rates and worse outcomes in 5 of 8 settings, but it does not identify why generated context harmed performance.
    contradiction_note: There is visible tension because one ablation found LLM-generated context improved performance when other repository documentation was removed.
    confidence: medium
  - claim_id: claim-003
    claim_text: Repository context files increased agent cost materially, with measured increases above 20% in the evaluated settings.
    claim_kind: bounded_claim
    evidence_ids:
      - evidence-04
      - evidence-09
      - evidence-11
    mechanism_or_bounds: The most supported mechanism is indirect and bounded: context files were associated with more agent steps, which likely contributed to higher cost, but the evidence does not fully decompose the cost increase.
    contradiction_note: No direct contradiction is visible on the direction of cost change; the main limit is that the size of the increase varies by context-file source and benchmark.
    confidence: high
  - claim_id: claim-004
    claim_text: The apparent usefulness of repository context depends on information conditions: developer-written files beat LLM-generated files in the main evaluations, but generated files helped when other documentation was removed.
    claim_kind: comparative_claim
    evidence_ids:
      - evidence-10
      - evidence-14
    mechanism_or_bounds: The evidence supports a conditional interpretation rather than a general mechanism: generated context may recover missing repository guidance in sparse-documentation settings, but this does not generalize to the main benchmark conditions.
    contradiction_note: This claim carries explicit tension: the main evaluations favor developer-written context across all four agents, while the documentation-removal ablation reverses that ordering.
